name: Pirc Defense
description: A hypermodern defense with d6 and g6. Black allows White's center then attacks it.

moves.commentary: The Pirc - a flexible defense that often leads to g6 and Bg7.

children.commentary: d4 stakes a claim in the center.
Nf6 develops and prepares the Pirc setup.
Nc3 develops and supports the center.
g6 prepares Bg7. The Pirc fianchetto.
Nf3 develops the knight to its natural square.
Bg7 completes the fianchetto. The Pirc Defense proper.
Be2 develops and prepares to castle.

strategy.keyIdea: Let White build a large center and then undermine it with piece pressure and well-timed pawn breaks — a hypermodern approach.
strategy.middleGamePlan: After fianchettoing the bishop on g7, use it as your main weapon against White's center. ...c5 and ...d6-d5 are the typical breaks depending on the setup. The g7 bishop is the cornerstone of Black's position — protect it and keep it active. Look for ...Bg4 to pin the knight and relieve pressure, and castle kingside to connect the rook with the g7 bishop.
strategy.watchOut: Allowing White to close the position and attack on the kingside before you've undermined the center — in the Pirc, timing of the counterattack is everything.
typicalGoals: Fianchetto the bishop on g7 as the main attacking piece
Play ...c5 to undermine White's d4 pawn
Use ...d5 as a pawn break when White's center overextends
Castle kingside and use the bishop-rook battery along the g-file

quiz.question: You're playing the Pirc. White has built a strong center with e4 and d4. What's your main weapon?

options.text: Exchange your fianchettoed bishop to remove a piece and simplify
options.explanation: The g7 bishop is the core of the Pirc — trading it voluntarily gives up your best piece and leaves you without compensation for allowing White's center.

options.text: Use the g7 bishop to exert long-range pressure and break with ...c5
options.explanation: Correct. The g7 bishop pressures White's center from the flank, and ...c5 is the typical undermining break that challenges d4 and creates counterplay.

options.text: Push ...e5 immediately to fight for the center directly
options.explanation: ...e5 without preparation is premature in the Pirc and can leave you with a weakened d6 pawn. Proper preparation with ...c5 or piece development comes first.

default.opponentMoveSummary: Your opponent stepped off the main theory line with an unusual move, choosing their own path rather than the expected response.
default.whyBookBreaks: The standard line assumes a particular pawn or piece configuration that this move bypasses.
default.opponentStrategy: Your opponent is trying to reach a less-charted position where their preparation or intuition gives them an edge.
forwardGuidance: Prioritize completing your development — get all minor pieces off the back rank.
Control the center with pawns or piece pressure before launching any flank operations.

f4.opponentMoveSummary: White played f4, expanding on the kingside immediately (Austrian Attack).
f4.whyBookBreaks: The Pirc main lines assume White plays d4, Nc3, and Nf3. f4 is the most aggressive deviation — immediate kingside expansion.
f4.opponentStrategy: Austrian Attack — White plays the most aggressive line, with immediate f4-f5 threat and kingside expansion.
forwardGuidance: Complete development quickly; White's f4 can become dangerous if you're passive.
Consider ...c5 or ...e5 to challenge the center; the Austrian demands active play.

Bg5.opponentMoveSummary: White played Bg5, pinning your knight and pressuring your kingside development.
Bg5.whyBookBreaks: The main line assumes d4, Nc3, Nf3. Bg5 pins the knight and restricts Black's options.
Bg5.opponentStrategy: Classical with pin — White pressures Black's kingside development and limits flexibility.
forwardGuidance: Consider ...h6 to challenge the bishop or develop around the pin.
Complete development; the pin is annoying but can often be resolved with ...Nbd7 or ...h6.

Be3.opponentMoveSummary: White played Be3, developing the bishop solidly and delaying commitment in the center (Byrne Variation).
Be3.whyBookBreaks: The main line assumes d4, Nc3, Nf3. Be3 develops solidly and keeps options open.
Be3.opponentStrategy: Byrne Variation — White develops solidly, delays committing the center, and aims for a flexible setup.
forwardGuidance: Complete your development; White's setup is solid but not aggressive.
Consider ...c5 or ...e5 to create counterplay when ready.
